title: From Symplectic Weyl Laws to Homeomorphism Groups and Beyond
speaker: Sobhan Seyfaddini
abstract:
The algebraic structure of various groups of homeomorphisms and diffeomorphisms was studied extensively in the 1960s and 1970s, when it was shown that these groups are (mostly) simple. A notable open case concerned the group of area-preserving homeomorphisms of the two-dimensional disc. We explain how invariants arising from Floer theory—an infinite-dimensional analogue of Morse theory—together with methods from continuous symplectic topology, show that simplicity fails in this case.
Symplectic Weyl laws play a central role in our story, as they permit the Calabi invariant—originally defined only in the smooth setting—to extend to area-preserving homeomorphisms. This, in turn, allows us to extend helicity, a conserved quantity of the three-dimensional Euler equations, from smooth volume-preserving flows to those that are merely continuous.

proof_stages.label: Fathi simplicity problem and nonsimplicity target
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the normal-subgroup problem for compactly supported volume-preserving homeomorphism groups, isolates the open area-preserving disk case, states nonsimplicity, and situates the question historically and motivationally.

proof_stages.label: C^0 symplectic bridge to homeomorphisms
proof_stages.summary: Symplectic manifolds, C^0 rigidity, and symplectic homeomorphisms are introduced to explain why area-preserving homeomorphisms of surfaces can be studied using symplectic topology.

proof_stages.label: Calabi homomorphism and topological extension problem
proof_stages.summary: The Calabi invariant is described as an average rotation homomorphism on smooth area-preserving disk diffeomorphisms, its extension to homeomorphisms is posed, the discontinuity and no-continuous-homomorphism obstructions are explained, and the theorem asserting many noncontinuous extensions is stated.

proof_stages.label: Floer spectral invariants and Weyl-law input
proof_stages.summary: A sequence of Floer-theoretic spectral invariants c_n is introduced, with three key properties: quasimorphism defect tending to zero, a Weyl law whose limit is Calabi, and C^0-continuity extending c_n to homeomorphisms.

proof_stages.label: Sequence-quotient homomorphism and Calabi extensions
proof_stages.summary: The invariants c_n are assembled into a homomorphism to real sequences modulo null sequences, yielding nonsimplicity without choice and, after choosing projections to the real line, many real-valued Calabi extensions and proper normal subgroups.

proof_stages.label: Helicity, Arnold linking formula, and Arnold's question
proof_stages.summary: Helicity of divergence-free vector fields is defined by differential forms, interpreted via Arnold's average asymptotic linking formula, and used to state Arnold's invariance and extension questions for homeomorphic conjugacies and C^0 volume-preserving flows.

proof_stages.label: Obstructions, hypotheses, and helicity theorem
proof_stages.summary: Naive differential-form and linking-number strategies are shown to break down for homeomorphisms, after which the main helicity theorem is stated under no-rest-point and no-positive-measure-periodic-orbit hypotheses and compared with prior and parallel results.

proof_stages.label: Plug insertion reduction to Calabi and helicity extensions
proof_stages.summary: Using volume-preserving flow boxes, area-preserving disk isotopies are inserted as plugs, the smooth change in helicity is identified with Calabi, and this identity is imposed with a chosen Calabi extension to define and characterize helicity extensions, including a universal abelianization-valued version.

examples.name: Fast rotation concentrated near a point
examples.note: A disk map with very large rotation localized near the center is used to show why Calabi is not C^0-continuous, forcing the proof away from a naive continuous-extension strategy.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses the fast-rotation-near-a-point picture to explain concretely why the Calabi invariant is not C0-continuous.

examples.name: Fast linking near a periodic orbit
examples.note: A flow whose nearby orbits link extremely rapidly around one periodic orbit illustrates how asymptotic linking can blow up and why direct C^0-continuity or Arnold-formula arguments fail.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker reasons through fast linking near a periodic orbit as a mechanism making asymptotic linking blow up and obstructing Arnold’s formula.

passes.reason: Rapid linking concentrated near one periodic orbit is presented as an obstruction to naive C0-continuity or Arnold-formula extension of helicity.

examples.name: Positive-volume periodic orbit via Hopf flow and Oxtoby-Ulam
examples.note: The speaker uses a pathological volume-preserving C^0 flow with a periodic orbit occupying most of the volume, later explained using Oxtoby-Ulam, to motivate the hypothesis excluding positive-measure periodic orbits.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker sketches the construction from the Hopf flow using Oxtoby–Ulam to make a periodic orbit carry positive volume.

examples.name: Convex-integration Euler solution with helicity loss
examples.note: A recent weak Euler solution for which vector-field-level helicity conservation fails is used as a contrasting example showing that the flow-level extension in the talk lives in a different low-regularity regime.
examples.engagement_reason: the convex-integration Euler solution with helicity loss is cited and its known irregularity/no-flow status is stated, but the construction is not worked through.
